examples.title: Algorithm: Disk Scheduling (SCAN)
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the SCAN (Elevator) disk scheduling algorithm that minimizes seek time by moving the head in one direction, servicing all requests, then reversing
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SCAN_Disk_Scheduling
INPUT: Request Queue (list of cylinder numbers), Initial Head Position, Disk Maximum Cylinder
OUTPUT: Total Seek Time, Sequence of servicing requests

BEGIN
 SET current_head = Initial_Head_Position
 SET direction = "right" // moving towards higher cylinder numbers
 SET total_seek_time = 0
 SET seek_sequence = EMPTY_LIST
 SET sorted_requests = SORT(Request_Queue in ascending order)
 
 // Find the starting index in sorted requests
 SET start_index = FIND_INDEX where sorted_requests[start_index] >= current_head
 
 // Service all requests to the right (higher cylinder numbers)
 FOR i FROM start_index TO length(sorted_requests)-1 DO
 SET cylinder = sorted_requests[i]
 SET seek_distance = ABS(cylinder - current_head)
 total_seek_time = total_seek_time + seek_distance
 current_head = cylinder
 APPEND cylinder TO seek_sequence
 END FOR
 
 // Reverse direction and go to the end of the disk
 IF start_index > 0 THEN
 total_seek_time = total_seek_time + (Disk_Max - current_head)
 current_head = Disk_Max
 END IF
 
 // Service all remaining requests to the left (lower cylinder numbers)
 FOR i FROM start_index-1 DOWN TO 0 DO
 SET cylinder = sorted_requests[i]
 SET seek_distance = ABS(cylinder - current_head)
 total_seek_time = total_seek_time + seek_distance
 current_head = cylinder
 APPEND cylinder TO seek_sequence
 END FOR
 
 OUTPUT total_seek_time
 OUTPUT seek_sequence
END
examples.explanation: The SCAN algorithm works like an elevator - the head starts at a position and moves in one direction (say toward higher cylinder numbers), servicing all requests in order. Once it reaches the end, it reverses direction and services remaining requests going back. This approach significantly reduces seek time compared to FCFS by avoiding random jumps across the disk.

examples.title: Python: Disk Scheduling Algorithms Implementation
examples.description: Complete Python implementation of FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, and C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithms with performance comparison
examples.code:
"""
Disk Scheduling Algorithms Implementation
Compares FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, and C-SCAN algorithms
"""

def calculate_seek_time(sequence, start):
 """Calculate total seek time given a service sequence"""
 total_seek = 0
 current = start
 for cylinder in sequence:
 total_seek += abs(cylinder - current)
 current = cylinder
 return total_seek


def fcfs_scheduling(requests, head):
 """First-Come, First-Served: Serves requests in arrival order"""
 sequence = requests.copy() # No reordering
 seek_time = calculate_seek_time(sequence, head)
 return sequence, seek_time


def sstf_scheduling(requests, head):
 """Shortest Seek Time First: Always pick closest request to head"""
 remaining = requests.copy()
 sequence = []
 current = head
 
 while remaining:
 # Find request closest to current head position
 closest = min(remaining, key=lambda x: abs(x - current))
 sequence.append(closest)
 remaining.remove(closest)
 current = closest
 
 seek_time = calculate_seek_time(sequence, head)
 return sequence, seek_time


def scan_scheduling(requests, head, disk_max=199):
 """SCAN (Elevator) Algorithm: Head moves in one direction, servicing
 all requests, then reverses"""
 sorted_requests = sorted(requests)
 sequence = []
 current = head
 
 # Split requests into those greater than head and those less/equal
 right = [r for r in sorted_requests if r >= head]
 left = [r for r in sorted_requests if r < head]
 
 # Service right side (toward higher cylinders)
 for cylinder in right:
 sequence.append(cylinder)
 current = cylinder
 
 # Go to end of disk and reverse
 if left: # Only if there are requests on the left
 current = disk_max # Jump to end
 
 # Service left side in reverse order
 for cylinder in reversed(left):
 sequence.append(cylinder)
 current = cylinder
 
 seek_time = calculate_seek_time(sequence, head)
 return sequence, seek_time


def cscan_scheduling(requests, head, disk_max=199):
 """C-SCAN (Circular SCAN): Head services in one direction only,
 then wraps around to beginning"""
 sorted_requests = sorted(requests)
 sequence = []
 current = head
 
 # Split requests
 right = [r for r in sorted_requests if r >= head]
 left = [r for r in sorted_requests if r < head]
 
 # Service right side
 for cylinder in right:
 sequence.append(cylinder)
 current = cylinder
 
 # Jump to beginning (no servicing on return)
 if left:
 current = 0 # Wrap around
 
 # Service left side
 for cylinder in left:
 sequence.append(cylinder)
 current = cylinder
 
 seek_time = calculate_seek_time(sequence, head)
 return sequence, seek_time


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Given example: requests for cylinders 95, 180, 35, 125, 15
 # Initial head position: 50
 requests = [95, 180, 35, 125, 15]
 head_start = 50
 disk_max = 199
 
 print("=" * 60)
 print("DISK SCHEDULING ALGORITHMS COMPARISON")
 print("=" * 60)
 print(f"Initial Head Position: {head_start}")
 print(f"Request Queue: {requests}")
 print(f"Disk Maximum Cylinder: {disk_max}")
 print("=" * 60)
 
 # Run all algorithms
 algorithms = [
 ("FCFS", fcfs_scheduling),
 ("SSTF", sstf_scheduling),
 ("SCAN", scan_scheduling),
 ("C-SCAN", cscan_scheduling)
 ]
 
 results = []
 for name, func in algorithms:
 sequence, seek_time = func(requests, head_start, disk_max)
 results.append((name, sequence, seek_time))
 print(f"\n{name} Algorithm:")
 print(f" Service Sequence: {sequence}")
 print(f" Total Seek Time: {seek_time} cylinders")
 
 # Summary
 print("\n" + "=" * 60)
 print("SUMMARY (Sorted by Performance)")
 print("=" * 60)
 results.sort(key=lambda x: x[2])
 for i, (name, _, seek_time) in enumerate(results, 1):
 print(f"{i}. {name}: {seek_time} cylinders")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates four major disk scheduling algorithms. FCFS serves requests in arrival order (simple but inefficient). SSTF always picks the closest request to minimize seek time but can cause starvation. SCAN moves like an elevator, servicing requests in one direction then reversing. C-SCAN services in one direction then wraps around to provide more uniform wait times. The example uses requests [95, 180, 35, 125, 15] with head starting at position 50.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) for SSTF (due to repeated search), O(n log n) for SCAN/C-SCAN (due to sorting), O(n) for FCFS
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing request queues and sequences

examples.title: C: RAID Level Simulation
examples.description: C program simulating RAID levels (0, 1, 5) with data striping, mirroring, and parity calculation
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates three RAID levels. RAID 0 uses striping (data distributed across disks in round-robin fashion) for performance but offers no redundancy. RAID 1 mirrors data on all disks for maximum redundancy at double the cost. RAID 5 distributes parity across all disks, allowing recovery from single disk failure by XORing remaining data. The program shows write operations, data distribution patterns, and how parity enables data recovery.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for read/write operations across RAID levels
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n × number_of_disks) for storing blocks across simulated disks
